When you write object literal syntax in JavaScript, you tend to make little mistakes along the way. The course called this out specifically, so let's go through it.
"Initialization" is really just the name of this chapter, but the actual content is mostly about the common mistakes you run into. For example...
As your object literals get bigger and bigger, it gets really easy to make mistakes — and those mistakes are exactly the kind of thing that scares beginners. Like this one:
And of course there's this one too (I make this one all the time):
Semicolon typed instead of equals sign

The process of these errors showing up is itself a form of initialization — because the engine is trying to initialize the object, and the syntax parser notices we wrote it wrong and throws an error message at us.

When you write object literals in JavaScript, it's really easy to slip up with a missing comma, a missing brace, or a typo like a semicolon where an equals sign should be. This post walks through those classic mistakes and why the syntax parser yells at you — which is all part of initialization.
